---
title: "10 · The Bellman Optimality Equation (BOE)"
subtitle: "Derive the BOE: value at s equals the max over actions of expected one-step return plus discounted next."
lesson-id: "mfrl.bellman-optimality-equation"
tier: "mfrl"
prerequisites: ["mfrl.optimal-policies"]
unlocks: []
applied-domains: ["ai-ml", "research"]
estimated-minutes: 30
status: "done"
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

Be honest. Are you ready for this lesson?

1. From [Lesson 6](../06-bellman-equation/index.qmd), can you write the
   policy-Bellman equation
   $v_\pi(s) = \sum_a \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a)[R + \gamma v_\pi(s')]$?
2. From [Lesson 9](../09-optimal-policies/index.qmd), can you state
   $v^*(s) = \max_\pi v_\pi(s)$ and explain why $\pi^*$ exists?
3. Are you comfortable with the operation $\max_a f(a)$ over a finite set
   of actions?

If a "no" appears, briefly revisit lessons 6 and 9.
:::

## Touch

Imagine the same grid world. The agent is at $s$. Above $s$, on a small
sticky note, are the four action values: $q^*(s, \text{up}) = 7$,
$q^*(s, \text{down}) = 5$, $q^*(s, \text{left}) = 6$, $q^*(s, \text{right}) = 3$.

What is $v^*(s)$? Pick the largest sticky note: $v^*(s) = 7$.

That is the entire content of the Bellman optimality equation. The optimal
policy puts all its weight on the action with the highest $q^*$. So
$v^*(s) = \max_a q^*(s, a)$ — the optimal value at $s$ is the *maximum*
expected return over actions, not an average.

```{ojs}
viewof showOpt = Inputs.toggle({label: "Show optimal pick"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  const qs = [7, 5, 6, 3];
  const labels = ["up", "down", "left", "right"];
  const opt = qs.indexOf(Math.max(...qs));
  const lines = ["Action sticky-notes at state s:"];
  for (let i = 0; i < 4; i++) {
    const marker = (showOpt && i === opt) ? "  ←★ optimal" : "";
    lines.push(`  q*(s, ${labels[i]}) = ${qs[i]}${marker}`);
  }
  if (showOpt) {
    lines.push(`v*(s) = max_a q*(s,a) = ${Math.max(...qs)}`);
    lines.push(`π*(s) = ${labels[opt]}`);
  }
  return html`<pre style="font-size:1rem;">${lines.join("\n")}</pre>`;
}
```

Caption: four action values at one cell. The optimal policy reads off the
largest. The BOE is the recursive form of "take the max."

## Wonder

1. The Bellman equation in lesson 6 had a $\sum_a \pi(a \mid s)$ averaging
   over the policy. The BOE replaces that with $\max_a$. What's the
   smallest *change* between the two equations?
2. The optimal policy must take *some* action at each state. Why is the
   optimal policy *deterministic* in finite MDPs?
3. Once $v^*$ replaces $v_\pi$, the equation becomes *non-linear* in
   $v^*$. Why does that matter for solving it?

## Discover

### Problem 1 — Start from the policy-Bellman for $\pi^*$

Take the Bellman equation from lesson 6 with $\pi = \pi^*$:
$$v^*(s) = \sum_a \pi^*(a \mid s) \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a)[R(s, a, s') + \gamma v^*(s')].$$
What does the inner sum represent for a fixed action $a$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
For a fixed $a$:
$\sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a)[R(s, a, s') + \gamma v^*(s')] = q^*(s, a)$.

This is the action-value formula from lesson 8 applied to $\pi^*$. So
the policy-Bellman equation for $\pi^*$ becomes
$v^*(s) = \sum_a \pi^*(a \mid s) q^*(s, a)$ — a policy-weighted average
of $q^*$.
</details>

### Problem 2 — Where does $\pi^*$ put its mass?

If $\pi^*$ is to *maximise* the average $\sum_a \pi^*(a \mid s) q^*(s, a)$
subject to $\pi^*(a \mid s) \ge 0$ and $\sum_a \pi^*(a \mid s) = 1$,
where should $\pi^*$ put its probability?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
A weighted average is maximised when *all* weight goes to the largest
input.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\pi^*(a \mid s) = 1$ for some $a \in \arg\max_a q^*(s, a)$, and 0
otherwise. (If multiple actions tie, $\pi^*$ can put all its mass on any
one of them — hence the non-uniqueness from lesson 9.)

The optimal policy is **deterministic**, supported on the argmax.
</details>

### Problem 3 — The BOE in $v$

Combine Problems 1 and 2: the average over $\pi^*$ of $q^*$ becomes the
*max* over $a$ of $q^*$. Write the result.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$$\boxed{\,v^*(s) = \max_{a} \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a)[R(s, a, s') + \gamma v^*(s')]\,}$$

This is the **Bellman optimality equation (BOE)** in $v^*$. One equation
per state. The unknown is $v^*(s)$ for every $s$.

Compare with lesson 6's Bellman equation:
$v_\pi(s) = \sum_a \pi(a \mid s) [\ldots]$.
Single change: the $\sum_a \pi(a \mid s)$ becomes $\max_a$.
</details>

### Problem 4 — The BOE in $q$

Now derive the analogous equation for $q^*$:
$$q^*(s, a) = \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) \!\left[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma \max_{a'} q^*(s', a') \right].$$

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Use $v^*(s') = \max_{a'} q^*(s', a')$ from Problem 2.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
From lesson 8: $q_\pi(s, a) = \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a)[R(s, a, s') + \gamma v_\pi(s')]$.
Set $\pi = \pi^*$:
$q^*(s, a) = \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a)[R(s, a, s') + \gamma v^*(s')] = \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a)[R + \gamma \max_{a'} q^*(s', a')]$.

The same shape as the policy-$q$ Bellman equation, with the inner
$\sum_{a'} \pi(a' \mid s')$ replaced by $\max_{a'}$.
</details>

### Problem 5 — Why the BOE is non-linear

The BOE in $v^*$ is
$v^*(s) = \max_a [\sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a)(R + \gamma v^*(s'))]$. Why is
this *not* a linear system in $v^*$? What does that imply for solving it?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$\max$ is not a linear operation: $\max(a + b, c + d) \ne \max(a, c) + \max(b, d)$ in general.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\max_a$ is non-linear. Two solutions $v_1, v_2$ to the BOE do not
combine linearly into a third — you cannot scale or add. The matrix-form
trick of lesson 7 ($v = (I - \gamma P^\pi)^{-1} r^\pi$) **fails** for
the BOE.

To solve the BOE we need the contraction-mapping theorem (lesson 11):
*iterate* the BOE operator from any starting vector and let it converge
to the unique fixed point. That's value iteration (lesson 13).
</details>

::: {.key-insight}
The BOE has the **same shape** as the Bellman equation from lesson 6,
with one tiny change: $\sum_a \pi(a \mid s)$ becomes $\max_a$. That
single substitution captures *the entire content of optimality*. It also
breaks linearity, which is why we need contraction arguments instead of
matrix inversion. The whole second half of this track exists to handle
that one $\max$.
:::

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

The **[Bellman optimality equation](/glossary.qmd#bellman-optimality-equation)
(BOE)** for a finite MDP with $\gamma < 1$ is, for every $s \in S$,
$$v^*(s) = \max_{a \in A} \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a)\!\left[R(s, a, s') + \gamma v^*(s')\right],$$
or equivalently $v^*(s) = \max_a q^*(s, a)$. In $q^*$ form,
$$q^*(s, a) = \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) \!\left[R(s, a, s') + \gamma \max_{a'} q^*(s', a')\right].$$
The BOE has a *unique* solution (lessons 11–12), and the greedy policy
$\pi^*(s) = \arg\max_a q^*(s, a)$ is optimal.

:::

A verse to remember it by:

> *Average becomes maximum;*
> *the policy collapses to argmax.*
> *Linear becomes non-linear;*
> *contraction the only way back.*

## Apply

### Worked example — solve the BOE for a 2-state MDP by inspection

States $\{X, Y\}$, two actions $\{a, b\}$. Transitions deterministic:

- $a$ at $X$ stays: $P(X \mid X, a) = 1$.
- $b$ at $X$ moves to $Y$: $P(Y \mid X, b) = 1$.
- $a$ at $Y$ moves to $X$: $P(X \mid Y, a) = 1$.
- $b$ at $Y$ stays: $P(Y \mid Y, b) = 1$.

Rewards: $R(X, b, Y) = 1$, $R(Y, a, X) = 1$, all others 0.
Discount $\gamma = 0.9$.

**Step 1 — write the BOE.**
$v^*(X) = \max\{\, 0 + 0.9 v^*(X), \quad 1 + 0.9 v^*(Y)\,\}$
$v^*(Y) = \max\{\, 1 + 0.9 v^*(X), \quad 0 + 0.9 v^*(Y)\,\}$

**Step 2 — guess the optimal action at each state.** "Move to the other
state" gives reward 1; "stay" gives 0. So the optimal policy probably
alternates: $\pi^*(X) = b, \pi^*(Y) = a$.

**Step 3 — under the guess, the BOE becomes the policy-Bellman.**
$v^*(X) = 1 + 0.9 v^*(Y)$
$v^*(Y) = 1 + 0.9 v^*(X)$.

By symmetry $v^*(X) = v^*(Y)$, call it $v$. Then $v = 1 + 0.9 v \Rightarrow v = 10$.

**Step 4 — verify the max.** $v^*(X) = 10$. Check the other action:
$0 + 0.9 \cdot 10 = 9 < 10$. Yes, $b$ wins. Symmetric for $Y$. The guess
was right.

**Step 5 — read $\pi^*$.** $\pi^*(X) = b, \pi^*(Y) = a$. Both states
worth $10$.

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** A 1-state MDP, two actions. $R(X, a, X) = 5$, $R(X, b, X) = 3$.
$\gamma = 0.9$. Write the BOE for $v^*(X)$ and solve.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$v^*(X) = \max\{5 + 0.9 v^*(X), 3 + 0.9 v^*(X)\} = 5 + 0.9 v^*(X)$
$\Rightarrow v^*(X) = 50$.

$\pi^*(X) = a$ (the higher-reward action).
</details>

**W2.** What is $v^*$ for a terminal state $s_T$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$v^*(s_T) = 0$. After termination there is no future reward to maximise.
</details>

**W3.** Modify W1 so the actions tie: $R(X, a, X) = R(X, b, X) = 5$. How
many optimal policies are there?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$v^*(X) = 50$ regardless. Both actions achieve the max. **Infinitely
many optimal policies** — any mixture of $a$ and $b$ works. $v^*$ is
unique; $\pi^*$ is not.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** Take the worked-example MDP and consider the *suboptimal* policy
$\pi(X) = a, \pi(Y) = b$ (always stay). Compute $v_\pi(X), v_\pi(Y)$
under $\gamma = 0.9$ and confirm $v_\pi \le v^*$ pointwise.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
"Always stay" gives reward 0 forever from either state. $v_\pi(X) = v_\pi(Y) = 0$.
Strictly less than $v^*(X) = v^*(Y) = 10$. The optimal policy
dominates as expected.
</details>

**P2.** Apply the BOE iteratively (one round): start from $v_0(s) = 0$,
compute $v_1(s) = \max_a [\text{reward} + 0.9 v_0(\text{next state})]$.
Then compute $v_2$ from $v_1$. Compare $v_2$ to $v^*$ for the worked
example.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$v_1(X) = \max\{0 + 0, 1 + 0\} = 1$. Continue.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Iteration 1.** $v_1(X) = \max\{0 + 0.9 \cdot 0, 1 + 0.9 \cdot 0\} = 1$.
$v_1(Y) = \max\{1 + 0.9 \cdot 0, 0 + 0.9 \cdot 0\} = 1$.

**Iteration 2.** $v_2(X) = \max\{0 + 0.9 \cdot 1, 1 + 0.9 \cdot 1\} = 1.9$.
$v_2(Y) = \max\{1 + 0.9 \cdot 1, 0 + 0.9 \cdot 1\} = 1.9$.

After 2 iterations $v_2 = (1.9, 1.9)$; the limit is $v^* = (10, 10)$.
Convergence is geometric — this preview of value iteration (lesson 13).
</details>

**P3.** *Sanity check the greedy.* For the worked example, compute $q^*(s, a)$
for all 4 (state, action) pairs from the optimal $v^* = (10, 10)$.
Confirm $\pi^*(s) = \arg\max_a q^*(s, a)$ matches your guess.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$q^*(s, a) = R(s, a, s') + \gamma v^*(s')$ where $s'$ is the deterministic
next state.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
- $q^*(X, a) = 0 + 0.9 \cdot 10 = 9$.
- $q^*(X, b) = 1 + 0.9 \cdot 10 = 10$.
- $q^*(Y, a) = 1 + 0.9 \cdot 10 = 10$.
- $q^*(Y, b) = 0 + 0.9 \cdot 10 = 9$.

$\arg\max_a q^*(X, a) = b$, $\arg\max_a q^*(Y, a) = a$. Same as Step 5
of the worked example. $\pi^*$ matches.
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** Show that the **BOE operator**
$T^* v(s) := \max_a \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a)[R + \gamma v(s')]$ is a
$\gamma$-contraction in $\ell_\infty$ norm:
$$\|T^* v - T^* w\|_\infty \le \gamma \|v - w\|_\infty.$$
(Hint: use the elementary fact $|\max_a f(a) - \max_a g(a)| \le \max_a |f(a) - g(a)|$.)

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
For each $s$, the difference $|T^* v(s) - T^* w(s)|$ is bounded by
$\max_a |Q_v(s, a) - Q_w(s, a)|$ where $Q_v(s, a) = \sum_{s'} P(\ldots)[R + \gamma v(s')]$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$|T^* v(s) - T^* w(s)| = |\max_a Q_v(s, a) - \max_a Q_w(s, a)| \le \max_a |Q_v(s, a) - Q_w(s, a)|$
$= \max_a \gamma |\sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a)(v(s') - w(s'))|$
$\le \gamma \max_a \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a)|v(s') - w(s')|$
$\le \gamma \|v - w\|_\infty \cdot \max_a \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) = \gamma \|v - w\|_\infty$.

Take $\sup_s$ on both sides: $\|T^* v - T^* w\|_\infty \le \gamma \|v - w\|_\infty$.
$T^*$ is a $\gamma$-contraction. **By Banach FPT (lesson 11)**, the BOE
has a unique solution and value iteration converges.
</details>

**S2.** Why does $\gamma = 1$ break the BOE story? (Two answers wanted:
algebraic and operational.)

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Algebraic: contraction constant becomes 1. Operational: returns can
diverge.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Algebraic.** $\|T^* v - T^* w\|_\infty \le \gamma \|v - w\|_\infty$.
At $\gamma = 1$, this becomes $\le 1 \cdot \|v - w\|_\infty$ — a
*non-strict* inequality. $T^*$ is no longer a contraction; the
fixed-point theorem fails to give uniqueness. There can be multiple
solutions or none.

**Operational.** With $\gamma = 1$, returns $G_t = \sum_k r_{t+k+1}$ can
be infinite, so $v^*$ may be undefined (or $+\infty$). Average-reward
formulations (Howard 1960) replace the discount with a different
optimisation criterion; the BOE story rebuilds with $h(s)$ relative
values instead of $v^*(s)$. Lessons 25-onward touch on this.
</details>

::: {.fun-fact}
The **single change** from Bellman equation to Bellman *optimality*
equation — $\sum_a \pi(a \mid s) \to \max_a$ — turns a linear system
into a non-linear one. Bellman called this "the principle of
optimality" in 1957. Critically, it also turns "easy to solve" (matrix
inverse) into "needs an iterative algorithm" — value iteration. So that
single $\max$ is the reason the entire contraction-mapping section of
this track exists. Without optimality, lessons 11–13 would be
unnecessary; with it, they are unavoidable.
:::

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"BOE is the policy-Bellman with $\pi = \pi^*$."** Subtly wrong.
   The policy-Bellman with $\pi = \pi^*$ is *linear in $v^*$*. The BOE
   uses $\max$, which is non-linear. The two equations have the same
   *solution*, but different *form*.
2. **"BOE always has a unique solution."** Yes, *for $\gamma < 1$ on a
   finite MDP*. At $\gamma = 1$ uniqueness can fail (S2).
3. **"BOE gives you $\pi^*$ directly."** No — it gives you $v^*$ (or
   $q^*$). The optimal policy is *extracted* afterward via
   $\pi^*(s) = \arg\max_a q^*(s, a)$.
:::

### Takeaways

1. The [BOE](/glossary.qmd#bellman-optimality-equation) replaces
   $\sum_a \pi(a \mid s)$ in the Bellman equation with $\max_a$. That
   single change captures all of optimality.
2. The BOE is **non-linear**: $\max$ does not interact with linear
   combinations. This is why we cannot solve it by matrix inversion.
3. The BOE operator $T^*$ is a $\gamma$-contraction in $\ell_\infty$.
   This is the seed of value iteration's convergence proof, which
   lesson 11 makes formal.

### Self-Assessment

Be honest with yourself.

1. Could you write the BOE in both $v^*$ and $q^*$ forms from memory and
   identify the single change from the policy-Bellman equation?
2. Did you finish W1–S1 without peeking past *Hint 1*?
3. Can you state in one sentence why solving the BOE requires
   contraction-mapping theory rather than linear algebra?

- **3 yes:** continue to lesson 11 (Banach fixed-point theorem) — already
  drafted, your next stop.
- **2 yes:** redo S1 — the contraction proof is the bridge to lessons
  11–13.
- **0–1 yes:** re-read *Discover*. Problem 5 captures why "max breaks
  linearity" matters.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **`mfrl.optimal-policies`** — for the existence of $\pi^*$ and the
  uniqueness of $v^*$, both used in deriving the BOE.

### What this unlocks

- **Lesson 11 — Contraction mapping theorem.** The tool we need to prove
  the BOE has a unique solution.
- **Lesson 12 — BOE uniqueness.** Apply contraction to the BOE
  specifically.
- **Lesson 13 — Value iteration.** Solve the BOE by iterating
  $v_{k+1} = T^* v_k$.
- **Lesson 26 — Q-learning.** A model-free, sample-based version of the
  BOE iteration in $q^*$.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**AI / ML.** Q-learning, DQN, AlphaZero's planning loop — all are
algorithmic descendants of the BOE. The $\max_{a'}$ inside Q-learning's
target is *literally* the BOE operator applied to the network's current
$q$-estimates.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Research / control.** The Hamilton–Jacobi–Bellman (HJB) equation in
continuous-time optimal control is the BOE's continuous analogue. The
$\max_a$ becomes a $\sup$ over an action set; the recursion becomes a
PDE; the rest of the structure is identical.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Sister synthesis lesson.** [`t7.rl-theory`](../../tier-7-masters-tracks/06-rl-theory/index.qmd)
gives the single-paragraph view; the BOE and its non-linear $\max$ are
the headline.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Author's notes

- Source: Shiyu Zhao, *Mathematical Foundations of Reinforcement Learning*
  (Springer 2025), Chapter 3 §3-4. Cite freely; never paste Zhao's prose.
- Touchstones honoured: Russian school (Discover *derives* the BOE in
  five problems), Better Explained ("$\sum$ becomes $\max$ — that's the
  whole change").
:::
